Notes
N/A
Good, more than adequate for most readers. Scholarship on details
sometimes outdated compared to more extensively researched
versions.
The "poet's Dante." Focuses more on effects and poetic license than
adhering to strict literal translation. Notes provide helpful guidance
to the text, and it is overall a very readable translation. Liberties are
taken to construct the pseudo-terza rima as well as get the sense of
Dante's words, as opposed to the exact meaning.
World of Dante
Basic. First time readers unfamiliar with of Dante might need
external aid to understand sections. Everyman (pictured) comes
with its own notes by one Peter Armour.
The "scholar's Dante." Strong focus on the Commedia's historical
context and influence of Virgil (Mandelbaum also did an acclaimed
of the
Aeneid).
translation
Good
scholarship
and
strong
textual/factual fidelity. The blank verse gives the work some
bounds, and will at times feel "stiff," but is still very readable.
Princeton Dante Project
Excellent and extensive. Very detail oriented, draws upon lots of
other commentators. Assumes basic familiarity with Dante. Very
scholarly. Very strong opinions on certain issues.
The "modern Dante." Extremely detailed notes, sometimes to the
point of exhaustion for readers who arent looking to get
comprehensive information on every single reference. Very literal
translation. Free verse could feel too abrupt and jarring at times.
Comments

Here my powers rest from their high fantasy.
but already I could feel my being turned-

by the Love that moves the sun and other stars.
Here my exalted vision lost its power.
But now my will and my desire, like wheels revolving
with an even motion, were turning with
the Love that moves the sun and all the other stars.

The notes are reason themselves to check out this volume. The
translation is "prose, as literal as possible," preserving a lot of
Dante's idiosyncratic syntax.
The result is a somewhat bumpy
reading experience that sacrifices flow and ease of reading at times
to remain as faithful as possible.
Legendarily influential and thorough. Commentary, interpretation,
noeuds.auteur: and information can all be found in these notes.

noeuds.titre: Comments
Another version that is distinguished by its notes. Notoriously bulky
(six volumes, two per cantica). The translation itself might feel
bland due to its age, but it's obvious a lot of care went into the
decisions Singleton made. Its Importance is evidenced by how
relevant and read it is even in light of the many excellent editions
that have become available since Singleton worked.

You're reading Sayers as much as Dante with these. Not necessarily
a bad thing, since Sayers' poetic powers are considerable, and there
is a strong impetus throughout this translation. Was very influential
some decades back, and could be interesting for fans to explore.
Some readers find it cliched and overwrought.
http://purgatorio.com/divine_comedy/intro.html
Adequate. Explains most common snags and references (some in
good depth), but not nearly as comprehensive as some alternatives.
Notes
A very well received contemporary translation. Pinsky is also a poet
by trade, and I believe he drew heavily on Yeats for the usage of
slant rhymes and consonants to bring Dante to English in the same
terza rima structure while preserving meaning and intent. Lots of
enjambment and mingling between stanzas/lines don't correspond
to the original exactly, which might tum off some readers.
This is, to my knowledge, the only Purgatorio-only translation. It is
obvious that Purgatorio left a profound impact on Merwin, who
spared none of his poetic powers (for this is another poet) to give
us this very eloquent and touching rendition. Very lyrical and hardly
ever stiff or stilted.

THROUGH ME THE ROAD TO THE CITY OF DESOLATION

THROUGH ME THE ROAD AMONG THE LOST CREATION.

WROUGHT ME. THE POWER, AND THE UNSEARCHABLY
HIGH WISDOM, AND THE PIRMAL LOVE SUPERNAL.
NOTHING ERE I WAS MADE WAS MADE TO BE
SAVE THINGS ETERNE, AND I ETERNE ABIDE

"Through me, into the city full of woe;
throuch me the message of eternal main:
through me, the message of eternal pain;
through me, the passage where the lost souls go.
Justice moved my Maker in his high domain;
Power Divine and Primal Love built me,
and Supreme Wisdom; I will aye remain.
Before me there was nothing made to be,
except eternity; eternal endure;
all hope abandon, ye who go through me."
THROUGH ME YOU ENTER INTO THE CITY OF WOES,
THROUGH ME YOU ENTER INTO ETERNAL PAIN.
THROUGH ME YOU ENTER INTO ETERNAL PAIN,
THROUGH ME YOU ENTER THE POPULATION OF LOSS.

WISDOM SUPREME, LOVE PRIMAL. NO THINGS WERE
BEFORE ME NOT ETERNAL; ETERNAL I REMAIN.
ABANDON ALL HOPE, YOU WHO ENTER HERE.

noeuds.titre: Translator
DANTE
A foreward/note on explaining the translation. No explanatory
notes for the text itself.
Arbitrarily oscillates between faithfulness and liberties, adding
extraneous content and removing others. Overall effect feel
trained, and the quatrain form sometimes makes the tercets fee
gourous. Often needlessly extends quatrains (there are
breaks separating stanzas, bleeds into each other) ineffective
Notes
Primarily dedicated to highlighting/explaining her anachronisms.
Deliberately modern and anachronistic, inserting references and
quotations from authors after Dante's time and pop culture. Overtly
concerned with offering something individualistic and unique.
"Destined to become an artifact of its era."

Historical
Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
Blank verse
Another popular historical translation, dominated America for some
time. Obviously reads stiff, though the cadence is decent and
there's a good poetic sense to it. Some awkward contractions and
Translator
Henry Francis Cary
Blank verse
First widely read English translation, often found even today in
public domain versions. There are obvious deficiencies when
looked at from a modern eye/compared to other versions, but is
common enough that I felt it merited inclusion.
John Sinclair
Prose, long paragraphs spanning multiple tercets
Its biggest claim to fame today is probably Harold Bloom's
endorsement of Sinclair as the best version, even in the face of
modern translations. It's unclear how up to date Bloom is with all
the new translations, so maybe take with a grain of salt. Singleton
likely borrowed heavily from Sinclair.

noeuds.auteur: I found myself within a forest dark,

noeuds.titre: In the middle of the journey of our life i came to myself within a
dark wood where the straight way was lost. Ah, how hard a thing it
is to tell of that wood, savage and harsh and dense, the thought of
which renews my fear! So bitter is it that death is hardly more. But
to give account of the good which I found there I will tell of the
other things I noted there.
Gates of Hell
Through me you pass into the city of woe:
Through me you pass into eternal pain:
Through me among the people lost for aye.
Justice the founder of my fabric mov'd:
To rear me was the task of power divine,
Supremest wisdom, and primeval love.
Before me things create werenone, save things
Eternal, and eternal 1 endure.
All hope abandon ye who enter here.
Through me the way is to the city dolent;
Through me the way is to eternal dole;
Through me the way among the people lost.
Justice incited my sublime Creator;
Created me divine Omnipotence,
The highest Wisdom and the primal Love.
Before me there were no created things,
Only eterne, and I eternal last.
All hope abaon, ye who enter in!
THROUGH ME THE WAY INTO THE WOEFUL CITY,
THROUGH ME THE WAY TO THE ETERNAL PAIN,
THROUGH ME THE WAY AMONG THE LOST PEOPLE.
JUSTICE MOVED MY MAKER ON HIGH,
DIVINE POWER MADE ME AND SUPREME WISDOM AND PRIMAL LOVE;
BEFORE ME NOTHING WAS CREATED BUT ETERNAL THINGS AND I ENDURE ETERNALLY.
ABANDON EVERY HOPE, YE THAT ENTER.

When he has fear, or when he is afflicted,
Not a drachm
Of blood remains in me, that does not tremble;
I know the traces of the ancient flame.
But us Virgilius of himself deprived
Had left, Virgilius, sweetest of all fathers,
Virgilius, to whom I for safety gave me:
Nor whatsoever lost the ancient mother
Availed my cheeks now purified from dew,
That weeping they should not again be darkned.
Dante, because Virgilius has departed
Do not weep yet, do not weep yet awhile;
For by another sword thou need'st must weep.
But ye the will roll'd onward, like a wheel

noeuds.titre: Translator
It (probably) looks like
DIVINE
DANTE
Cantos start with summary and end with a mix of notes and brief
commentary. Very reader and newbie friendly, but nothing too
groundbreaking or novel.
Followed Dorothy Sayers in the Penguin line-up. Simple, unadorned
verse that tries to let Dante stay at the forefront (a complete 180
from Sayers). A fine enough version, but not particularly standout or
memorable torme
Nice notes. Not as thorough as some others that specialize in it, but
also offers commentary alongside just mere explanatory notes. Has
a little something for both novices and veterans.
The most recent Penguin edition, replaced Mark Musa's. The blank
verse is readable and is at times almost like prose. It's a good
translation for sure, and one could do much worse than start with
Kirkpatrick, but it's also not necessarily super distinguished from
the more established versions that do similar things.
votes
Comments
Opening
Midway along the journey of our life
I woke to find myself in a dark wood,
for i had wandered off from the straight path.
IAM THE WAY INTO THE DOLEFUL CITY,
I AM THE WAY INTO ETERNAL GRIEF,
TAM THE WAY TO A FORSAKEN RACE.
JUSTICE TI WAS THAT MOVED MY GREAT CREATOR;
DIVINE OMNIPOTENCE CREATED ME,
AND HIGHEST WISDOM JOINED WITH PRIMAL LOVE.
BEFORE ME NOTHING BUT ETERNAL THINGS
WERE MADE, AND I SHALL LAST ETERNALLY.
ABANDON EVERY HOPE, ALL YOU WHO ENTER
At one point midway on our path in life,
noeuds.auteur: I came around and found myself now searching

noeuds.titre: "Through me you go to the grief-wracked city.
Through me to everlasting pain you go.
Through me you go and pass among lost souls.
Justice inspired my exalted Creator.
I am a creature of the Holiest Power,
of Wisdom in the Highest and of Primal Love.
Nothing till I was made was made, only
eternal beings. And I endure eternally.
Surrender as you enter every hope you have."
I turned aside (and leftwards) meaning now;
with all the hope and deference of some child
that runs when hurt or frightened to its mum,
There is not one gram
of blood in me that does not tremble now.
I recognize the signs of ancient flame.'
But Virgil was not there. Our lack alone
was left where once he'd been. Virgil, dear sire,
Virgil - to him l'd run to save my soul.
Nor could the All our primal mother lost,
ensure my cheeks - which he once washed with dew-
should not again be sullied with dark tears.
"Dante, that Virgil is no longer here,
do not yet weep, do not yet weep for that.
A different sword cut, first, must make you weep."
Gates of Hell
Virgil - Beatrice - Dante [I] turned to the left-with all the confidence
(Purgatorio 30.43 - 57)
that makes a child run to its mother's arms,
when he is frightened or needs comforting.
"Not one drop of blood
is left inside my veins that does not throb:
I recognize signs of the ancient flame."
But Virgil was not there. We found ourselves
without Virgil, sweet father, Virgil to whom
for my salvation i gave up my soul.
All the delights around me, which were lost
by our first mother, could not keep my cheeks,
once washed with dew, from being stained with tears.
"Dante, though Virgil leaves you, do not weep,
not yet, that is, for you shall have to weep
from yet another wound. Do not weep yet."
At this point power failed high fantasy
but, like a wheel in perfect balance turning,
I felt my will and my desire impelled
by the Love that moves the sun and the other stars.
All powers of high imagining here failed.
But now my will and my desire were turned,
as wheels that move in equilibrium,
by love that moves the sun and other stars.
Ending
Ultimately, I really don't think it matters. Everyone freaks out about "ong which of the 100 translations should I read?" when it really doesn't change anything for
99% of readers. If you really want to get deep into Dante I hope this chart is helpful for you. About me: I don't speak Italian. I've read most of these translations in full, and parts of
others (Singleton, Sinclair, Sayers). I don't claim particular deep expertise or scholarship on Dante, but I think I'm more invested than the casual/one-time reader. Email 4chandantechart@gmail
if you have any comments/suggestions.

This is VERSION 1.0, and it is INCOMPLETE. It is kinda ugly and probably has errors and typos. It is missing text from the Singleton and Sinclair versions. If you have copies of them/the relevant excepts, PLEASE EMAIL ME.
